title: The Power of Scale for Parameter-Efficient Prompt Tuning
authors: Brian Lester, Rami Al-Rfou, Noah Constant
hook: Tiny learned prompts can steer huge language models to new tasks without retraining.
promise: You will understand how prompt tuning adapts large language models efficiently using only small learned prompts.
prerequisite_note: You just need a sense for how language models use prompts to guide their outputs.

glossary.plain: Learning a small set of special input tokens to guide a frozen model for a new task.
A prompt made of learned vectors, not actual words, optimized by gradient descent.
A model whose main parameters are not updated during training on a new task.
Achieving good performance by changing only a tiny fraction of the model's parameters.
Adapting to a new task using only a few labeled examples, typically by crafting text prompts.
Updating all of a model's parameters to adapt it to a new task.
A related method that prepends learnable vectors to the input but is more complex than prompt tuning.

cards.title: Steering Giants with Tiny Prompts
body: See how small learned prompts can adapt enormous language models for new tasks, without updating the whole model.

cards.title: How can you change a giant model with a tiny nudge?
body: Imagine having a massive language model, trained for months, that you want to repurpose for a new task. Retraining it would be slow and expensive.
What if you could simply teach it a new trick by learning a small add-on prompt, leaving the rest of the model untouched? This is the magic of prompt tuning.

cards.title: From frozen model to flexible tool
body: Start with a frozen, pre-trained language model that already knows a lot about language.
Learn a short, task-specific prompt as a set of new vectors by training only those, not the whole model.
The model, guided by this prompt, produces outputs tailored to the new task almost as well as if it had been fully retrained.
cards.caption: Prompt tuning adapts a single frozen model to many tasks with tiny, learned prompts.

cards.title: Full model tuning versus prompt tuning
body: Pretend you need to adapt a big model for sentiment analysis. Which method do you choose?
cards.offNote: Retrain all parameters, needs lots of compute, slow to deploy and store.
cards.onNote: Only learns a short prompt, fast and cheap, keeps the main model unchanged.

cards.title: Learning prompts, not weights
body: Instead of updating the billions of parameters in a language model, prompt tuning learns a small set of new input vectors that act as a soft prompt.
These soft prompts are optimized using gradient descent, allowing the frozen model to solve new tasks efficiently by conditioning on these learned cues.

cards.title: What happens as models get bigger?
body: Slide to see how prompt tuning's effectiveness changes as the underlying model size increases.
cards.low: Prompt tuning lags behind full tuning, not enough model capacity.
cards.mid: Prompt tuning catches up, performance gap narrows.
cards.high: Prompt tuning matches full tuning, efficient adaptation at scale.

cards.title: What goes into prompt tuning?
body: Select the ingredients needed to adapt a frozen model using prompt tuning.

choices.label: Frozen language model
choices.note: Provides the base knowledge, remains unchanged.

choices.label: Labeled examples
choices.note: Guide the learning of the prompt for each task.

choices.label: Downstream task objective
choices.note: Defines what counts as success for the new task.

choices.label: No main model updates
choices.note: Ensures only the prompt is learned, not the entire model.

cards.title: Prompt tuning closes the gap at scale
body: Prompt tuning outperforms classic few-shot prompting and, for very large models, matches the performance of full model tuning.
This means that with a huge model, you only need to learn a tiny prompt to get top results on new tasks, saving time and resources.
cards.caption: With bigger models, prompt tuning closes in on full model tuning performance.

cards.title: A new way to adapt big models
body: Prompt tuning showed that you do not need to retrain massive models for every task. A small, learned prompt is enough.
This approach opened the door to sharing and serving large models more efficiently, making them much more flexible in practice.

recap.label: Learn
recap.note: Train a small, soft prompt for each task.

recap.label: Condition
recap.note: Feed the prompt to guide the model's output.

recap.label: Adapt
recap.note: Achieve near full-tuning performance with minimal updates.

cards.title: The Power of Scale for Parameter-Efficient Prompt Tuning
body: Read the full paper for details, ablations, and comparisons to other parameter-efficient adaptation methods.
